data.title: ‘Get Your Shopping Lists Ready’: Morgan Stanley Suggests 2 Stocks to Buy as Market Weakness Could Create Opportunity
data.description:
The war with Iran has become a clear backdrop to the market’s recent weakness. Rising tensions in the region bring a familiar set of worries – geopolitical instability, possible disruptions to global oil supplies, and the broader uncertainty that tends to follow war. None of that tends to do equities any favors.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

But that’s not the whole story. Mike Wilson, chief US equity strategist from Morgan Stanley, noted that liquidity was tightening and that markets were shifting toward correction territory. In his opinion, the current war has simply brought the stressors into sharper focus. That said, Wilson believes that investors can find opportunities in the near term as the market bottoms out this spring.

“The questions for equity investors now are what will the world look like in six months and are prices cheap enough to start assuming a better future? The short answer is not yet, but get your shopping lists ready… I think the S&P 500 could trade toward 6300 by early April before our favorable fundamental outlook can take hold again,” Wilson opined.

Wilson’s is the long-term upbeat view, and his colleagues among the Morgan Stanley stock analysts are busy picking out stocks to buy in a weaker market environment. We’ve opened up the TipRanks platform to look up the details on two of those picks – here they are, along with the Morgan Stanley commentaries.

Flywire Corporation (FLYW)

We’ll start in the world of online payment processing, where Flywire has, for over 15 years, offered a wide range of online digital payment solutions. The company operates around the world, providing services for the education, travel, and hospital/health sectors. Flywire’s platform lets international companies operate smoothly in a multi-currency global environment, taking payments in their customers’ currencies and using their own local currencies to settle their own accounts.

The company works with more than 4,900 global clients, and its service covers more than 240 countries and territories and over 140 currencies. At its core, the Flywire service is a desirable combination of vital advantages: secure transactions, low payment costs, and simplified reconciliations.

In an important development, Flywire last year acquired Sertifi, the cloud-based online electronic signature platform. The acquisition cost Flywire $330 million, and Sertifi provided $14.2 million in revenue for Flywire during 4Q25.

The popularity of Flywire’s service can be seen in a key metric from the company’s 4Q25 report. In that release, Flywire reported that its total payment volume increased by 35.6% year-over-year, from $6.9 billion in 4Q24 to $9.3 billion in the final quarter of 2025.

Elsewhere in the 4Q25 report, Flywire reported generating $157.5 million at the top line in the quarter, for a 34% year-over-year increase and beating the estimates by $7.74 million. At the bottom line, Flywire’s GAAP EPS came to $0.00 – and that figure beat the forecast by a penny. During Q4, Flywire paid down the remaining debt, $15 million, which it had incurred in the Sertifi acquisition.

Morgan Stanley analyst Michael Infante sees Flywire in a sound position, based on recent performance. He writes of the company, “We think Flywire has earned back credibility in the public markets, with four straight quarters of MSD+ RLAS beats, and with the ’26 outlook still assuming meaningful visa headwinds, we think the stage is set for continued upward estimate revisions, which should warrant multiple expansion… In our view, the best evidence of Flywire’s share gain and cross-sell success is the spread between visa growth and revenue growth in key Education markets, as Flywire’s results in ’25 demonstrate the company’s ability to deliver robust results in a fairly punitive visa regime.”

Quantifying his stance, Infante rates the shares as Overweight (i.e., Buy), and sets a price target of $17 that suggests a one-year upside potential of 36%. (To watch Infante’s track record, click here)

The 12 recent analyst reviews here break down to 7 Buys, 4 Holds, and 1 Sell, for a Moderate Buy consensus rating. The stock is priced at $12.49 and its $16.60 average target price indicates a 33% upside in store for the coming year. (See FLYW stock forecast)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: 2 ‘Perfect 10’ Stocks Analysts Think Are Strong Buys
data.description:
Every investor aims to find the perfect stock, but locating the shares that check every box is no easy task. The stock market throws up a massive wall of information. Thousands of traders are dealing in thousands of companies, generating millions of transactions each day. The result is an imposing mountain of data that can make separating signal from noise a real challenge.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Fortunately, investors don’t have to tackle that mountain of information alone. TipRanks’ Smart Score harnesses AI and natural language processing to sift through the market’s vast data universe, organizing and interpreting it in a way that helps investors make smarter decisions.

The system analyzes each stock using a set of proven factors that historically correlate with future outperformance. These signals are then distilled into a single, easy-to-read rating on a scale from 1 to 10. A “Perfect 10” suggests that multiple data-driven indicators are lining up in the stock’s favor – a strong signal that the name may deserve a closer look. In this way, the Smart Score turns stock selection into something closer to a science than a guessing game.

With that in mind, we’ve opened up the TipRanks database to look for stocks that carry that Perfect 10 score – and not only that, they have also earned ‘Strong Buy’ support from Wall Street’s analysts. Here are two stocks that stand out.

JBS (JBS)

We don’t think about it as often as it deserves, but food is big business. The first Perfect 10 stock on our radar today, JBS, is a $16 billion player in the global food industry, and a world-leading supplier in several areas. The company boasts that it is the #1 global producer for both poultry and beef, and the #2 global producer for pork. On a regional level, JBS is the #1 egg producer in South America, the #2 salmon producer in Australia, and the #3 plant-based foods producer in Europe.

JBS was founded in Brazil in 1953, and today operates headquarters in both Sao Paulo and Amsterdam. The company employs over 280,000 people globally, operates more than 250 production facilities worldwide, and has sales relationships with customers in over 180 countries.

The food industry can be a major source of pollution in the world’s environments, and JBS makes a point of addressing these issues at the source. The company has a commitment to promoting sustainable agriculture and supply chains, starting with stewarding natural resources at farms, and then sourcing responsible commodities. This is followed by setting up sustainable food production chains – all to allow for stronger food supply systems. The goal is to reduce the environmental impact while still feeding the global population.

JBS last reported earnings for 3Q25; its next report, covering 4Q25, is due out later this month. The company’s quarterly net sales in Q3 came to $22.6 billion, for a 13% year-over-year increase. JBS’s bottom line, the EPS of 52 cents, was down 16% from 3Q24, although it beat the forecast by a nickel.

UBS analyst Matheus Enfeldt is upbeat about JBS and sees it as a potential winner going forward. He says, “We expect JBS to appreciate as it continues to rerate following the US listing and joins indices in the country, a process which typically lasts for 2+ years. The cherry on top of the cake is that we believe returns from company’s ~US$1bn annual expansion capex remain unpriced by the market, which may add ~1% upside in FCFEy per year vs what is priced in, and given JBS’s solid growth track record we see some upside is warranted. We acknowledge buying into cycle weakness may be challenging, however the micro side of the story is sufficiently strong to deliver stock performance in our view.”

Enfeldt follows up these comments with a Buy rating for the stock and a $19.50 price target that suggests a share price gain of 28% in the coming months.

The Strong Buy consensus rating here is unanimous, based on 5 recent positive analyst reviews. JBS is trading at a price of $15.21, and its $20.75 average price target indicates room for a gain of 36% on the one-year horizon. (See JBS stock forecast)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: Up to 10% Dividend Yield: Analysts Pick 2 Dividend Stocks to Buy
data.description:
Is the war really about to be over? That’s what recent statements from the White House might suggest – and investors are paying close attention.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

On Monday evening, speaking to reporters, President Donald Trump said bluntly, “We’re achieving major strides toward completing our military objective,” adding that “the war is very complete, pretty much.”

However, investors have learned over the past year that policy direction from the White House can change abruptly. Trump has shown a tendency to zigzag on major economic and geopolitical decisions. The tariff saga last April offered a clear example, when trade policy shifted back and forth in short order, leaving markets struggling to keep pace.

That pattern is precisely why many investors prefer not to rely on a single headline when positioning their portfolios. Even if the conflict appears to be nearing its conclusion, the broader environment can still change quickly – whether through renewed tensions, shifting trade policy, or other unexpected developments.

With that in mind, investors with a defensive mindset are gravitating toward dividend stocks.

Dividend payers offer three advantages that become especially valuable during uncertain periods. First, they tend to provide relative stability, as companies that consistently distribute cash to shareholders are often mature businesses with durable cash flows. Second, dividends generate a steady stream of income that can help cushion portfolios during volatile market swings. Third, they can serve as a hedge against inflation, since many dividend-paying companies increase their payouts over time as earnings grow.

Wall Street’s analysts would seem to agree; some of the top-rated stock experts have been tagging high-yield dividend payers as stocks to buy. These are shares with high dividends – or potential dividends – with yields that can climb to 10%. We’ve opened the TipRanks database to get a closer look at these picks; here is what we’ve found.

ARKO Petroleum (APC)

We’ll start with ARKO Petroleum, a spin-off from ARKO Corporation. The Richmond-based ARKO Corporation is a major operator of convenience stores across the US, and until February of this year, it was also an important wholesaler of gasoline and other fuels. At that time, the company spun off its fuel distribution business as ARKO Petroleum Corp – and ARKO Petroleum Corp took that business public. APC shares started trading on February 12, and the offering closed the next day. The company put 11,111,111 shares on the market at $18 each, and raised $200 million in gross proceeds from the IPO event. ARKO Petroleum, as a newly public company, has a market cap of $679 million.

Looking at the business, we see that ARKO Petroleum is well-positioned across most regions of the US: the Mid-Atlantic, Midwest, Northeast, Southeast, and Southwest. The company supplies fuel to customers in over 30 states, through fee-based wholesale distribution. ARKO Petroleum deals mainly in motor fuels, and sells to retail locations operated by the ARKO parent company. It also deals with third-party fuel dealers under long-term contractual agreements.

As a public company, one of ARKO Petroleum’s key objectives is to make regular quarterly cash distributions for its shareholders – that is, to pay dividends – and to increase that distribution over time. Management has already indicated its intent to pay out a $0.50 quarterly dividend; at that rate, the dividend will yield 10% and will position ARKO Petroleum as a strong income-generating equity.

UBS’s 5-star analyst Josh Silverstein likes this new stock, citing its growing business – and its dividend potential. He writes, “Our positive outlook is driven by APC’s volume growth, improving margins, dividend yield, and B/S strength. We forecast APC’s gallons sold growing at a 5% CAGR, lifting Adj. EBITDA from FY25’s $143mm to $191mm in FY28. At APC’s 65-70% cash conversion rate, this drives $13mm of incremental DCF/yr, supporting 5% annual dividend growth… We see potential 2026 acquisitions solidifying the growth outlook and driving a move to our PT, which implies a 9% yield… We see APC growing its dividend at 5%/yr. This growth outlook is in line with peers, while it minimizes the pressure growth & acquisition capex will place on the B/S.”

Silverstein rates APC as a Buy, and sets a $22 price target that suggests a one-year upside potential of 13%. If the dividend potential holds up, the one-year return on this stock could reach as high as 23%. (To watch Silverstein’s track record, click here)

Since going public, ARKO Petroleum has picked up 5 analyst reviews – and the 4 to 1 split, favoring Buys over Holds, gives a Strong Buy consensus rating. The stock is currently trading for $19.40, and its $21.60 average price target indicates an 11% upside for the coming year. (See APC stock forecast)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: ‘Buy Energy’: UBS Suggests 2 Energy Stocks to Consider Amid Geopolitical Risk
data.description:
The Middle East has erupted into active warfare over the past eleven days, and all eyes are turning to the Strait of Hormuz. This narrow waterway links the oil- and gas-rich countries of the Persian Gulf to global shipping routes and is widely considered one of the most critical energy chokepoints in the world. Roughly 20% of the world’s hydrocarbon supplies pass through the strait, meaning any disruption there can quickly ripple across global energy markets.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Oil prices reacted immediately as the conflict intensified. Brent crude briefly surged to nearly $120 per barrel on Monday – its highest level since the months following Russia’s 2022 invasion of Ukraine – while U.S. benchmark WTI climbed to a similar level. Yet, prices reversed sharply later in the day after President Donald Trump suggested the conflict could be nearing its conclusion. Following those remarks, Brent and WTI tumbled back below $90 per barrel by late trading.

Even after that strong reversal, crude remains far above the roughly $70 per barrel range where it traded before the U.S. and Israel launched their campaign against Iran on February 28.

Against this backdrop, the energy analysts at UBS have been closely monitoring the situation and its implications for the energy sector. The bank believes the current setup still favors energy stocks and argues that geopolitical risk may not yet be fully reflected in market pricing.

“We increase PTs and continue to see a positive risk/reward for Energy… The longer the Middle East conflict goes on, the more upside we see to the entire curve. We do not believe the stocks and forward curve reflect that risk, as both have moved <3% since Feb 20th, the Friday before the conflict. We also see continued upward pressure on global natural gas prices due to potential Qatar supply concerns. Based on this setup, combo oil/nat gas companies potentially have the most FCF upside,” the team wrote.

With that in mind, the UBS analysts have singled out two energy stocks they believe are particularly well positioned to benefit if oil prices remain elevated. Using the TipRanks database, we took a closer look at how these picks stack up against the broader Wall Street consensus. Let’s dive in.

Magnolia Oil & Gas (MGY)

We’ll start in Houston, Texas, where Magnolia Oil & Gas operates as an independent producer in the hydrocarbon field. The company’s footprint is in the southeastern part of the state, specifically in the areas of Giddings and Karnes; the company’s Karnes holdings are considered a ‘coveted’ location, in the core of the Eagle Ford Shale formation.

What this means is that Magnolia works in some of Texas’ richest oil and gas regions. The company had an average daily production in 4Q25 of 103.8 Mboe/d; this figure indicated that the company’s production accelerated during the year – for the full year 2025, average daily production came to 99.8 Mboe/d. The Q4 production figure was up 11% from the final quarter of 2024. Production in the Giddings area, where Magnolia has its most extensive holdings, represented 79% of the total. Magnolia is targeting 5% production growth for 2026.

Like many energy producers, Magnolia pays out a reliable dividend to its investors. The company has been gradually raising that payment since 2022; the last declaration, made on January 29, was for a $0.165 payment on March 2 to common shareholders. At that rate, the dividend annualizes to 66 cents per common share and gives a forward yield of 2.3%.

Turning to the financials, in 4Q25, Magnolia reported $317.6 million in quarterly revenue. This was down 2.7% year-over-year, but it did beat the forecast by $3.89 million. The bulk of this revenue, over $215 million, came from oil production. Natural gas and natural gas liquids made up the remainder. Magnolia reported a 4Q diluted EPS of 37 cents; this was down 16% from the prior-year period, although it came in $0.01 ahead of the estimates.

UBS analyst Peyton Dorne counts several reasons why investors should get on board here. Dorne writes, “We lift our MGY PT from $29 to $35, largely reflecting our increase in our 2027E EV/EBITDAX PT multiple from 4.85x to 5.75x. This is a premium to the 5.0x 5-year average multiple, which we see as warranted due to the higher near-term oil price levels, MGY’s underlying inventory growth, and its successful track record in pairing shareholder returns with underlying volume growth. MGY remains our Top Pick amongst SMID Cap E&Ps because of its durable capital returns, balance sheet strength, and top tier capital efficiency, demonstrated by its peer leading reinvestment rate.”

These comments support the analyst’s Buy rating on the shares, while the $35 price target implies a one-year upside potential of ~20.5%. (To watch Dorne’s track record, click here)

The 16 recent analyst reviews on Magnolia split 9 to 7 in favor of Buy over Hold, all for a Moderate Buy consensus rating. It should be noted that shares are up by over 33% so far this year. As such, the $29.15 average price target suggests shares will stay rangebound for the time being. With this mind, watch out for either more price target hikes or rating downgrades shortly. (See MGY stock forecast)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: ‘Look Past the Noise’: Oppenheimer Suggests 2 Stocks to Buy Despite Global Tensions
data.description:
The past week’s biggest development was the escalation of hostilities in the Middle East, as the U.S. and Israel launched a new campaign targeting Iran’s nuclear program and its network of regional proxies. Military conflicts often rattle markets in the short term, typically weighing on sentiment.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

However, Oppenheimer chief investment strategist John Stoltzfus argues that markets have historically absorbed geopolitical shocks relatively quickly.

“From a historical perspective we recall that most downside market reactions to outbreaks of hostilities tied to military actions or risks of military actions when they have occurred around the world tend to be relatively short-lived. As counterintuitive as that might seem to inexperienced market participants and observers in actuality once markets factor in the risks of events of immediate concern, they consider the need for restocking of defense equipment and rebuilding as well as the normal day to day needs of humanity after which markets tend to rally sometimes with sizeable strength on consideration that life and the demands it necessitates will persevere,” Stoltzfus opined.

With that backdrop in mind, Oppenheimer’s stock analysts are looking past the near-term turmoil and identifying opportunities they believe could reward investors willing to stay focused on the bigger picture. Several names stand out as potential buys right now, including two stocks that the Oppenheimer analysts believe offer compelling upside. We turned to the TipRanks database to see whether other analysts share that view.

CoreWeave (CRWV)

The first Oppenheimer pick we’re looking at here is CoreWeave, a New Jersey-based AI infrastructure company that was founded in 2017 – and in its short 8-and-a-half-year life, the company has built itself into a $38 billion platform provider. CoreWeave gives its customers access to a top-tier software platform, cloud-based, that is fully able to power the best current AI products and services – and has potential to support the next generation as well. CoreWeave built its platform on reliable and resilient GPU clusters, and can support any AI workloads, at any scale.

CoreWeave’s platform is described by the company as AI-native, and is built for high-speed applications. The company’s solutions, built into the platform, include AI training and reinforcement learning; agentic AI and agent development; and even VFX rendering. CoreWeave has built the latest security systems into the platform, from the ‘silicon up.’

Along with all of this, CoreWeave offers products designed for data and storage; infrastructure control; mission control; and runtime acceleration. CoreWeave’s cloud platform holds a leading position on the AI frontier, with fast compute times and seamless orchestration built to promote and accelerate AI breakthroughs.

AI has quickly become a big business, and CoreWeave’s most recent financial report, from fiscal 4Q25, shows some of this. The company went public in March of last year, and this last report marked the fourth earnings release as a publicly traded firm. The company has seen its revenues increase sequentially in each report.

The 4Q top line came to $1.57 billion, for a 110% gain year-over-year – and beating the forecast by $40 million. The company ran a net loss, however; the EPS for 4Q25 came to ($0.89), a loss that was much deeper than the ($0.34) reported in the prior-year period and fell short of the estimates by $0.21.

Oppenheimer’s Param Singh covers this stock, and is upbeat for a number of reasons. Laying out those reasons, Singh says, “Our bullish thesis for CoreWeave is predicated on: (1) the outsized TAM opportunity for AI-optimized IaaS; (2) its strong competitive positioning against large hyperscalers/cloud vendors, and other neo-clouds; and (3) its ability to deliver sustainable high FCF margin upon reaching maturity. We believe investor concerns with near-term CapEx/debt requirements are short-sighted, and as CoreWeave exits hyper-growth, cash return should improve and re-rate the stock higher.”

Along with these comments, Singh puts an Outperform (Buy) rating on the stock – and a price target of $140 that suggests a 92% upside potential for the coming 12 months. (To watch Singh’s track record, click here)

The Moderate Buy consensus rating on this stock is based on 21 recent reviews that include 12 Buys, 8 Holds, and 1 Sell. The stock’s share price of $72.99 and average target price of $116.16 together imply a one-year gain of 59% for the stock. (See CRWV stock forecast)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: Goldman Sachs Sees Market Warning Signs – But Likes These 2 Stocks
data.description:
Is everything old new again? We had a financial crisis in 2008, and while market moods are better than that these days, some analysts are starting to see some disturbing parallels.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

That’s the view of Goldman Sachs strategist Peter Oppenheimer, who notes that risk premia in the equity markets are down and are running at levels we haven’t seen since the run-up to the 2008 crisis. While that might present a presentiment of the bad old days, Oppenheimer isn’t all doom-and-gloom; in a recent note, he warns that what we’re looking at is more in line with a correction than a full-out bear market.

While risk may be increasing, the Goldman Sachs stock analysts still see shares worth buying. We’ve opened up the TipRanks data platform to look at two stocks which the firm likes, even in today’s environment. Let’s give them a closer look and find out why Goldman Sachs is recommending Buys.

American International Group (AIG)

We’ll start in the insurance and underwriting space, with AIG. This company has been around for over a century, and today its global platform is available in more than 200 countries. AIG wrote $23.9 billion in net premiums in 2024, and another $23.8 billion in 2025. The company operates through three main segments: North American Commercial, International Commercial, and Global Personal.

Among the insurance products available through AIG are property and casualty policies, accident and health policies, and financial, life, and liability coverage. AIG also has financial services such as retirement solutions and asset protection. It’s all related to risk management and future security.

AIG pays out a regular dividend to its shareholders, and its last declaration, made on February 10, set a March 30 payout of a 45-cent common share payment. At that rate, the dividend annualizes to $1.80 per common share and gives a 2.25% forward yield. Last year, AIG returned $6.8 billion worth of capital to its shareholders; this return was made through $5.8 billion in share repurchases, and $1 billion in dividend payments.

In its 4Q25 report, released in February, AIG reported $670 million in general insurance underwriting income, a total that was up 48% from the prior-year period. The bottom line reported a non-GAAP adjusted after-tax income per diluted share of $1.96, for a 51% year-over-year gain and beating the forecast by 6 cents per share.

Goldman analyst Robert Cox says of this insurance giant, “We see AIG as a company with peer-high earnings growth and an improving ROE over the coming years (similar to CB), while peers will generally face decelerating ROEs and slower earnings’ growth due to cyclical pressures. While we do not think AIG is immune by any means to cyclical pressures, AIG’s earnings growth is supported by a strong underwriting position, recent accretive deals that should increase operating and premium leverage, and capital flexibility given ongoing sell-downs of its CRBG stake.”

He goes on to add about the company’s prospects, “AIG’s 1.1x P/B (10.4x P/E) valuation is attractive relative to an ROE regression, especially considering our estimates for the trajectory of stronger fundamentals relative to peers. Furthermore, AIG appears amongst the best positioned as it relates to artificial intelligence and other headline risk (including affordability) as an underwriter focused primarily on large account, multi-national, commercial insurance underwriting, and appears to be an AI investment first-mover based on company commentary and disclosure.”

All told, these comments support Cox’s Buy rating, while his $90 price target implies a one-year upside potential of 12%. (To watch Cox’s track record, click here.)

Turning to the general Street view, this stock gets a Moderate Buy consensus rating, based on 17 recent reviews, including 5 Buys and 12 Holds. The shares are trading for $80.07 and the $87 average target price indicates a 9% gain in the year ahead. (See AIG stock forecast.)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: ‘Valuation Extremely Inexpensive,’ Says Top Analyst After Broadcom (AVGO) Earnings Address Investor Concerns
data.description:
Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) is hogging the limelight in Thursday’s trading with the shares pushing higher after the chip giant delivered fiscal first-quarter results that showed the semi giant’s strong growth trajectory remains intact.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Broadcom generated revenue of $19.31 billion (up 29.4% year-over-year) and adj. EPS of $2.05, slightly ahead of Street expectations of $19.26 billion and $2.03. Semiconductor revenue came in at $12.52 billion vs. the Street’s $12.31 billion forecast, while software revenue was a touch below at $6.80 billion vs. $6.86 billion. The standout was AI-focused semiconductors, which generated around $8.4 billion, up 106% YoY and beating the Street’s $8.2 billion estimate. Gross margins came in at 77.0%, down roughly 90 basis points quarter-over-quarter but slightly above expectations, while operating expenses were lower than expected.

Looking ahead, the FQ2 guide impressed, driven by an optimistic AI outlook. Revenue is expected to reach $22.0 billion with EBITDA margins of 68% and adj. EPS of ~$2.35, well above Street estimates of $20.5 billion, 66.4%, and $2.16. Semiconductors are expected at $14.8 billion, including AI sales of approximately $10.7 billion – up $2.3 billion sequentially and $1.5 billion above Street estimates. Gross margins are expected to remain near 77%, just above consensus, and software revenue is projected at about $7.2 billion, growing modestly QoQ and roughly in line with expectations.

Bernstein’s Stacy Rasgon, an analyst who ranks among the top 2% on Wall Street, hailed a solid print, backed by a “very strong” guide, and thinks the company “knocked down (at least) four outstanding investor concerns” on the call.

First, management reassured investors worried about the previous ~$73 billion AI order outlook by signaling a clear path to nearly 10 GW of AI shipments and over $100 billion in AI revenue by FY27. Second, the company noted that gross margin dilution from AI rack sales is not expected to be significant. Third, investors have been concerned about “customer-owned tooling” and the possibility of rising competition. Management pushed back on that view, arguing the risk is limited given the company’s technological capabilities and rapid pace of innovation. Finally, management addressed investor concerns that AI could disrupt the company’s software business, a fear that has recently weighed on sentiment. They argued the opposite – that their software stack, particularly VMware Cloud Foundation, represents a critical infrastructure layer for running AI workloads.

After a strong quarter and an encouraging outlook for AI-driven growth, Broadcom’s fundamentals are looking very robust and at a decent price to boot. “It now appears that EPS approaching $20 a share could conceivably be in the realm of possibility for next year,” said the 5-star analyst, “putting valuation extremely inexpensive relative to likely earnings power, with accompanying margins and free cash flow entering rarefied territory.”

Accordingly, Rasgon maintained an Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares and raised his price target from $475 to $525, implying the stock will appreciate by 58% over the one-year timeframe. (To watch Rasgon’s track record, click here)

Most other analysts are on the same page; based on a mix of 29 Buys vs. 2 Holds, AVGO receives a Strong Buy consensus rating. Going by the $457.89 average target, 12 months from now, investors will be pocketing returns of 38%. (See AVGO stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘Simply the Best’: Nvidia Stock Gets New Street-High Price Target – Shares Could Almost Double
data.description:
At a conference on Wednesday, Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said demand for the company’s products was “higher than very high.”

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Such a statement might sound hyperbolic, but that claim probably won’t come as much of a surprise to Tigress Financial’s Ivan Feinseth, who has just increased his price target for NVDA from $350 to a new Street-high of $360, suggesting the shares will rise by 97% over the coming months. Naturally, Feinseth’s rating stays a Buy. (To watch Feinseth’s track record, click here)

Simply put, Nvidia is at the forefront of GPU accelerators and full systems for AI training and inference, serving hyperscalers, enterprises, and sovereign AI projects. Its Data Center business has become the major revenue driver and is still “growing rapidly.” As Blackwell “ramps into volume” for these same customers, and future architectures like Rubin/Vera Rubin and Feynman reduce inference costs and increase throughput, Feinseth says Nvidia “sustains an ongoing AI capital investment cycle that widens its moat and supports premium pricing.”

At the same time, the company’s ecosystem – including CUDA, cuDNN, and a wide range of AI frameworks and SDKs – supports millions of developers and thousands of enterprise applications, creating high switching costs and strong customer loyalty. On the enterprise side, offerings like Nvidia AI Enterprise and vertical stacks for healthcare, industrial digital twins, and robotics generate recurring software and service revenue alongside hardware sales.

Feinseth also points out that platforms like Omniverse and industrial digital twins further fuel GPU and AI demand in design, simulation, and factory optimization, linking real-world applications back to the data center. Meanwhile, Nvidia’s DRIVE and robotics platforms bring AI to the edge, and sovereign AI projects expand its reach into automotive, logistics, and national compute infrastructure, broadening the company’s total addressable market.

There’s so much money being spent on AI-related endeavors, and much of it is heading Nvidia’s way. Aggregate capital investment from leading hyperscalers and cloud providers is expected to exceed $650 billion in 2026, with Nvidia capturing a significant portion through its industry-leading GPUs. The total AI market is expected to reach $3–$4 trillion by 2030 as hyperscalers, enterprises, and sovereigns reconfigure data centers into AI “factories.”

Moreover, high-value networking and system attachments – like NVLink, NVL72, Spectrum‑X, and InfiniBand – along with hardware-software co-design and the CUDA/AI Enterprise ecosystem, create “deep platform lock-in.”

“Combined with exceptional free‑cash‑flow generation, disciplined reinvestment in R&D and capacity, and robust capital returns through buybacks and dividends, these growth engines drive rising returns on capital and expanding economic Profit, translating into accelerating long‑term shareholder value creation,” the 5-star analyst summed up.

So, that’s the Tigress view and it is one shared by most other analysts. All told, the stock claims a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on a mix of 39 Buys vs. 1 Hold and Sell, each. The forecast calls for 12-month returns of 48%, considering the average target clocks in at $272.16. (See Nvidia stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Sell Rivian (RIVN) Shares Because Its Expectations Are Too High, Says BofA
data.description:
Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN) investors are hoping the release of its more affordable mid-size SUV, the R2, will be a game-changer for the company, but it is unlikely to have the desired effect.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

That is the opinion of BofA analyst Alexander Perry, who believes the electric vehicle maker will find it hard to gain traction in the current environment.

Perry anticipates that EV adoption will remain relatively modest, which could limit near-term profit growth amid a challenging regulatory backdrop, including the expiration of federal tax credits and the rollback of CAFÉ and greenhouse gas standards that previously supported regulatory credit revenue. Even under a more supportive regulatory framework, Perry believes U.S. consumer uptake of EVs has lagged expectations due to concerns over affordability, driving range, charging availability, and low resale values.

Perry sees overall EV penetration falling to 6% of light vehicle sales in 2026, down from 8% in 2025, due to the phase-out of EV incentive programs and a reduced focus on EVs by traditional automakers, particularly Ford and General Motors. The analyst’s forecast implies a more than 20% drop in industry EV sales, contrasting with Rivian’s call for a 53% increase in deliveries (including the R2 model, flat if excluding it), which would suggest market share gains while competitors shift toward higher-margin internal combustion engine vehicles and scale back premium EV programs.

Perry does think the R2 launch will broaden Rivian’s addressable market, noting that roughly 69% of U.S. EVs sold in 2025 were priced under $50K, or about 845,000 units. That said, competition in this price segment is intensifying, with Tesla introducing more affordable Model 3/Y standard variants ($37K/$40K), priced 13%/11% below their original models, Lucid planning a mid-size platform under $50K in 2027 – 37% below its current Gravity Platform – and Ford aiming for more affordable EVs built on its universal platform starting in 2027.

The analyst also believes Rivian is rapidly progressing in its autonomous driving technology, though he notes it still trails leading player Tesla.

All that is to say Perry reinstated coverage of RIVN stock with an Underperform (i.e., Sell) rating, backed by a $14 price objective, implying shares will decline by 6% over the one-year timeframe. (To watch Perry’s track record, click here)

Perry says his bearish take could be invalidated should Rivian rollout additional autonomy features, which could increase deliveries – especially point-to-point capabilities later this year – and by stronger-than-expected demand for the R2 given its more affordable $45K price point.

5 other analysts join Perry in the bear camp, but with an additional 8 Buys and Holds, each, the stock claims a Hold consensus rating. Going by the $17.32 average target, a year from now, shares will be changing hands for a 16% premium. (See RIVN stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: GM and Ford: Bank of America Names 2 Top Auto Stocks to Buy for 2026
data.description:
The changeover of Presidential Administrations always brings changes of policy priorities, and those can ripple outward. Sometimes, it’s impossible to know where the ripples will end, and sometimes, it’s easy. Last month, one of the easier ripples made itself felt.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The second Trump Administration took office promising to roll back large parts of the Biden regulatory regime, including difficult findings on greenhouse gases and automobile fuel economy. The greenhouse gas standard, the GHG, is a rollback, meaning that the Feds have rescinded their designation of carbon dioxide as a ‘threat to public health and welfare.’ The fuel economy measure concerns CAFE, or corporate average fuel economy, which is used to set gas mileage and efficiency standards for cars on the road. Trump’s Administration has lowered the standard, making it easier for automakers to comply – the higher Biden standard was designed to promote construction of electric vehicles.

This new regulatory model will allow Detroit’s major automakers, Ford and GM, to lean harder into high-margin trucks and SUVs, their big money-makers, while dialing back orders for less popular electric vehicles.

Bank of America is following this conclusion to its logical end: that the Detroit legacy names are going to benefit from this chance to change course under a more favorable regulatory environment. The bank is naming General Motors (NYSE:GM) and Ford (NYSE:F) – two legendary names in the automotive industry – as top auto stocks for 2026. Let’s give them a closer look, and find out why these veterans of Detroit’s Big Three are getting Bank of America’s thumbs-up.

General Motors

We’ll start with General Motors, the owner of the Chevy, Buick, Cadillac, and GMC nameplates. These include some highly recognizable names, such as the Chevy Silverado pickup truck and the Cadillac Escalade SUV. GM has been selling industry-leading cars for over a century; the company was formed as a holding company in 1908, and its first brand acquisition was Buick. The company today boasts a $71 billion market cap, and in 2025 claimed a market share of the US automotive industry in excess of 17%.

A look at some numbers will show how strong GM’s position is. Last year, the company’s total auto sales came to 2.85 million vehicles, a figure that was up 6% year-over-year; two of GM’s full-size pickups, the Chevy Silverado and the GMC Sierra, led the American market for the sixth consecutive year, with combined sales of 940,000 units. GM is also doing well in the lower-end parts of the market, and last year sold almost 700,000 Buick and Chevy cars priced below $30,000.

It’s a known fact that electric vehicle sales have been declining in recent months – the Financial Times reports that the world’s auto industry has absorbed a $65 billion hit over the past year from paring back its EV production – but internal combustion engine vehicles are rising. GM, last month, announced a $46 million investment at an assembly plant in Ontario, Canada – a plant that builds Chevy Silverado trucks.

When we turn to the direct financial results, we find that GM finished 2025 with $45.3 billion in revenue for the fourth quarter, down 5% year-over-year. The fourth-quarter figure missed the forecast by $750 million. At the same time, earnings were sound – the non-GAAP EPS, at $2.51, was up some 30% year-over-year, and exceeded the forecast by 25 cents per share.

Looking ahead for this automaker, Bank of America’s Alexander Perry sees a strong future. The industry expert writes, “We believe GM is a key beneficiary of recent regulatory changes including the removal of CAFÉ penalties, and GHG relief, which is enabling a mix shift to its most margin accretive trucks/SUVs and away from unprofitable EVs. The evidence is compelling: we estimate that trucks/SUVs variable profit per unit is $17.5K vs. corporate average of $10-12K. We also believe GM should benefit in 2026 from lower warranty costs & regulatory credits, lean inventory levels, and low incentives.”

With these potential gains in mind, Perry rates GM as a Buy, with a $105 price target that implies a one-year upside of 34%. (To watch Perry’s track record, click here.)

GM’s shares are currently trading for $78.60; the average price target, at $95.76, suggests a gain of 22% for the year ahead. The stock’s Moderate Buy consensus rating is based on 19 recent recommendations that include 14 Buys, 4 Holds, and 1 Sell. (See GM stock forecast.)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: Will Apple’s (AAPL) Portfolio Refresh Catalyze Revenue? Daniel Ives Thinks So
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) has been singled out as an AI laggard, but it is doubling down on hardware upgrades designed to improve AI performance across its ecosystem.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The company has rolled out a broad set of new products spanning the iPhone, Mac, iPad, displays, and chips.

At the center of the launch are the new M5 Pro and M5 Max chips for MacBook Pro, built on Apple’s Fusion Architecture, which combines two dies into a single system-on-chip. Compared to the previous M4 generation, the new chips deliver more than 4x peak GPU compute for AI workloads and boost graphics performance by 35%.

The company also introduced an updated MacBook Air, along with a new entry-level laptop called the MacBook Neo. The Air and Pro run on the new M5 chips, while the Neo uses the A18 Pro chip to improve AI performance, memory capacity, and battery life. The latter is a more affordable laptop priced at $599 ($499 for education), offers 16 hours of battery life, 256GB of storage, and up to 50% faster performance in everyday tasks, while delivering roughly 3x faster on-device AI processing.

With nearly half of Mac buyers still new to the platform, Wedbush analyst Daniel Ives believes the updated portfolio could provide incremental upside to Mac estimates by appealing to a broad range of consumers looking to upgrade their laptops.

Apple also introduced the iPhone 17e, powered by the new A19 chip and priced around $599, offering 256GB of storage, all-day battery life, and improved on-device AI performance. The company also refreshed the iPad Air with the M4 chip, delivering about 30% faster performance, and launched two updated Studio Display models with stronger connectivity and brighter 5K displays aimed at professional workflows. “We believe that the launch of its new products (at good price points) lowers the risk profile around Apple’s revenue growth profile over the coming quarters as the company launches a wide array of products that can leverage AI workflows across the product suite,” Ives went on to say.

While nicely priced new products are welcome, against a backdrop of investor concerns about rising memory costs, Apple also raised prices on several new products. Both updated MacBooks come with higher ASPs, with the MacBook Air now starting at $1,099 for the 13-inch model (up from $999) and $1,299 for the 15-inch (up from $1,199), while the MacBook Pro starts at $2,199 (up from $1,999), as Apple passes higher memory costs on to consumers to help protect margins. “We believe that putting higher ASPs across its portfolio is the right move given the uncertainty around memory’s impact on product gross margins as AAPL looks to navigate greater complexities across its supply chain,” Ives further said.

Bottom line, Ives maintained an Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating on AAPL stock, backed by a Street-high $350 price target. The implication for investors? Upside of 33% from current levels. (To watch Ives’ track record, click here)

Elsewhere on the Street, AAPL stock claims an additional 14 Buys, 9 Holds and 1 Sell, for a Moderate Buy consensus rating. The forecast calls for 12-month returns of 17%, considering the average target clocks in at $306.07. (See Apple stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘A Winner in Autonomous Driving,’ BofA Pounds the Table on Tesla Stock
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) investors are banking on the company succeeding in the autonomous driving era, and they have good reason to be optimistic.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

So thinks BofA analyst Alexander Perry, who believes the company is better positioned than its peers on this front. “We expect TSLA to quickly become a leader in robotaxi services, given its ability to scale more profitably than competitors,” Perry said. “We see autonomous vehicles spurring the next era of mobility and as the most significant change agent in the Auto 2.0 landscape, offering consumers the prospect of saving time, safer travel, and more accessible transportation.”

For Perry, the Robotaxi represents the “most significant opportunity.” The company is set to expand its Robotaxi service, which is already running in San Francisco and Austin, to seven more markets in the first half of 2026. While most autonomous vehicle companies rely on multi-sensor setups combining LiDAR, radar, and cameras, Tesla uses a camera-only system. This method is technically more challenging but significantly less expensive and benefits from the company’s extensive consumer-fleet data. Perry thinks Tesla’s approach will allow it to “scale more profitably,” while its driver-free model provides a clear cost advantage over traditional rideshare services.

Meanwhile, Tesla’s FSD (full self-driving) software is still in the early stages of generating revenue, but Perry sees it as the “leading consumer autonomy solution,” offering the most advanced point-to-point capabilities. Currently, Tesla has 1.1 million FSD subscriptions, representing roughly 12% of its fleet, with a target of 10 million subscriptions linked to a stock award plan for CEO Elon Musk. Perry expects adoption will accelerate as Tesla “optimizes its offering for higher levels of autonomy.”

Then there’s the Optimus humanoid robot, expected to see initial adoption in manufacturing, potentially replacing part of the roughly 13 million U.S. manufacturing jobs, with longer-term use in households. Tesla’s Energy business also gets a mention, with Perry anticipating it will maintain a leading position through residential Powerwall batteries and Megapacks deployed by utilities and data centers.

And what about the core auto business? Here, Perry thinks Tesla could capture 7% of the global automotive market over the long term, positioning it among the world’s top five automakers. This represents growth from an estimated 1.8% market share in 2025, according to S&P data, and would surpass current legacy players such as General Motors (4.8%) and Ford (4.5%). At this level, Tesla could reach approximately 7 million vehicle sales per year.

“Overall,” Perry summed up, “we find that TSLA’s core automotive business represents around 21% of the total value while robotaxi is 52%, FSD is 19%, Energy Generation & Storage is around 6% and Optimus is 2%.”

Added up, that value results in a price objective of $460, a figure suggesting 12-month upside of 13%. Perry has reinstated coverage of TSLA with a Buy rating. (To watch Perry’s track record, click here)

12 other analysts join Perry in the bull camp while an additional 11 Holds and 7 Sells add up to a Hold consensus rating. However, the $399.25 average target implies shares will stay rangebound for the time being. (See Tesla stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Strategy Stock Has Price Target Slashed – But Is Still a Buy, Says Clear Street
data.description:
Bitcoin seems to be staging a recovery at the moment, up by 7% over the past day, so it’s no surprise to find Strategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) shares are also on the move, up by 11% at the time of writing.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The gain in Wednesday’s session should boost sentiment among investors, as the stock has endured a brutal pullback, shedding 59% over the past 6 months.

The depressed price action mirrors the general downbeat trajectory Bitcoin has taken and has led Clear Street analyst Brian Dobson to revise his outlook. Reflecting “persistently lower BTC prices and more conservative BTC yield assumptions,” Dobson has lowered his target from $268 to $198, although the revised figure still makes room for 12-month appreciation of 34%. Dobson’s rating stays a Buy. (To watch Dobson’s track record, click here)

While Dobson is less optimistic on the near-term action, he keeps an overall bullish stance following a recent meeting with Strategy’s executive chairman and co-founder Michael Saylor and president and CEO Phong Le. “We continue to view MicroStrategy as a highly differentiated and scalable vehicle for Bitcoin exposure,” said Dobson. “The company’s treasury strategy has proven durable, with BTC holdings now exceeding 673k BTC. While volatility remains elevated, we believe disciplined balance sheet management, a growing NAV base, and consistent growth in Bitcoin per share remain core drivers of long-term value creation.”

As for key takeaways from the meeting, Dobson says the company plans to lean into preferred share issuance in the first half of 2026, using “structured capital” to fund additional BTC purchases while preserving “equity optionality” and strengthening STRC’s “institutional positioning.” STRC refers to Strategy’s perpetual preferred stock that pays variable dividends, has no maturity, and was created to raise capital efficiently. Management emphasized that STRC is the company’s strategic priority and a cornerstone of its next phase in capital markets development. The goal isn’t just to hold BTC, but to build an institutional-grade instrument that offers deep, liquid trading, lower volatility than spot BTC, and operates as a fully regulated crypto credit vehicle.

On the regulatory front, management sees the next major milestone coming after passage of the Clarity Act: a potential US withdrawal from the Basel framework, which currently imposes steep capital charges on BTC holdings, effectively discouraging meaningful balance sheet exposure. “While likely more than a year away,” Dobson said, “reform could materially improve BTC adoption among legacy financial institutions.”

The company’s thesis is that BTC is evolving from a speculative asset into a “sovereign-adjacent reserve asset.” As institutional and sovereign capital shifts from gold to BTC, its scale grows, volatility drops, and it becomes “balance sheet infrastructure.” At that stage, a BTC credit sector can emerge, and MSTR aims to be the “benchmark BTC credit issuer.”

“Strategically,” Dobson added, “MSTR is evolving from BTC proxy to BTC treasury to BTC-backed credit platform, with STRC serving as the bridge toward sovereign-grade crypto capital market infrastructure.”

Turning now to the rest of the Street, where there have been 9 other recent MSTR reviews, all also positive, making the consensus view a Strong Buy. At $310, the average target points toward one-year returns of 111%. (See MSTR stock forecast)

Disclaimer: The opinions expressed in this article are solely those of the featured analyst. The content is intended to be used for informational purposes only. It is very important to do your own analysis before making any investment.
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Broadcom Earnings Are About to Hit – Here’s Why HSBC Has Lowered Its Price Target
data.description:
Once the market action comes to a halt today, Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) will deliver its fiscal first-quarter report.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

HSBC analyst Frank Lee expects a solid print, anticipating the company will report revenue of $19.5 billion, slightly above management’s guidance of $19.1 billion and the consensus estimate of $19.2 billion. The analyst sees adj. EPS reaching $2.05, just above consensus at $2.02.

Looking ahead to 2QFY26, Lee is a bit more optimistic than the Street, too, expecting stronger AI networking revenue. While his ASIC revenue projections match consensus, his AI networking estimates are about 30% higher, which lifts overall AI revenue by roughly 8%. That drives his 2QFY26 revenue forecast to $21.0 billion, about 3% above the Street at $20.3 billion. Lee expects adj. EPS of $2.23, about 3% ahead of consensus at $2.17.

Yet while Lee’s estimates are generally above Street forecasts, ahead of the print he has maintained a Buy on the shares, yet has lowered his price target from $530 to $450, now suggesting the stock will gain 40% in the year ahead. (To watch Lee’s track record, click here)

So, what’s behind the reduced target? Well, even though sell-side analysts have steadily raised their revenue forecasts since Broadcom’s 4QFY25 results, the stock hasn’t been able to re-rate. Since that earnings announcement, the stock has lagged the broader market, dropping 22% compared with a 1% fall for the S&P 500 and a 4.5% decline for the NASDAQ over the same period. Lee believes this underperformance isn’t due to any change in fundamentals but reflects growing market caution toward major AI compute players.

Lee thinks the market’s caution is down to three main factors. First, AI compute players rely heavily on hyperscaler capex, which is rising rapidly – 2026e spending for the Big Five hyperscalers (Microsoft, Amazon, Meta, Alphabet, Oracle) is $680 billion, up $160 billion in just three months. The concern is whether hyperscalers can sustain such high capex given limited AI monetization, especially as recent funding moves by Alphabet and Oracle highlight potential cash flow pressures.

Second, the rise of agentic AI is shifting demand toward server CPUs, raising questions about whether traditional AI accelerators may lose share during the “booming inference phase.”

Third, ASIC growth appears to have stalled, with few new customer wins or additional CoWoS capacity beyond Alphabet’s TPU deployments.

“Due to the above-mentioned market concerns, we believe that the PE premium that the market was initially giving the AI compute players has been diminishing − resetting the overall valuation framework,” Lee summed up.

Like Lee, however, the rest of the Street still remains in Broadcom’s corner. The stock claims a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on 28 Buys vs. 2 Holds. At $449.46, the average target closely matches Lee’s objective. (See AVGO stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Buy Nvidia Shares Ahead of GTC, Says Wells Fargo
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) stock might be showing little momentum right now but if history is anything to go by, that might all change soon.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The chip giant’s upcoming GTC 2026 event is scheduled for March 16‑19. Historically, the stock has tended to perform strongly after GTC, with Nvidia typically outperforming the SOX index by around 30% over the three months following the event, ranging from roughly 12% to 45% over the past few years.

Hence, says Wells Fargo analyst Aaron Rakers: “We’re NVDA buyers ahead of the event.”

Among the items Rakers will be looking out for are updates regarding the pipeline. With commitments already exceeding $500 billion and potentially stretching into calendar 2027, Rakers estimates Nvidia has recognized roughly $240 billion to $250 billion of that opportunity since Blackwell began shipping in late 2024. An update to $600 billion+ would be a positive.

Additionally, Rakers, who ranks among the top 1% of analysts on Wall Street, will be watching for updates on Nvidia’s move to the Kyber architecture – its next-generation, high-density rack design – and how lessons from the early GB200 NVL72 rollout might shape that transition. He’ll also be looking to see whether Feynman, Nvidia’s planned post-Rubin GPU architecture, represents a completely new approach or just an evolution of existing designs.

Rakers will also be keeping an eye on Rubin CPX, Nvidia’s GPU built for long‑context AI inference and expected to launch in late 2026, as well as how the company positions its Groq LPU for AI workloads. He’s looking for more detail on Rubin CPX’s architecture and how Nvidia plans to integrate Groq’s technology, particularly its large on-chip SRAM and streaming design that helps keep inference fast and low-latency.

There could be some “wildcards” thrown in the mix too. The 5-star analyst points out that AI infrastructure growth reflects Jevons Paradox – as compute gets more powerful, usage expands, driving more token generation. Within this context, Groq integration vs. new inference-focused silicon could be in focus. There has been speculation about whether Nvidia’s Groq IP agreement will lead to standalone chips or if the technology will be incorporated into the company’s future GPU roadmap.

Another wildcard focuses on the prospect of customization. AMD + Meta’s recent chiplet-based partnership highlights the potential for custom AI hardware for hyperscalers. Nvidia’s Blackwell and Rubin GPUs already use a chiplet/dielet architecture, with Vera-Rubin Ultra expanding this further. The Wall Street Journal has reported that Nvidia plans a dedicated inference chip for OpenAI, leveraging its Groq IP license to optimize AI inference. According to the report, OpenAI has committed to significant purchases, making customized AI hardware a potential wildcard for Nvidia’s competitive positioning.

All told, Rakers maintained an Overweight (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares, backed by a $265 price target. The implication for investors? Upside of 47% from current levels. (To watch Rakers’ track record, click here)

38 other analysts join Rakers in the bull camp, overpowering 1 Hold and Sell, each, and all adding up to a Strong Buy consensus rating. The forecast calls for one-year returns of 51%, considering the average target clocks in at $271.89. (See Nvidia stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Wells Fargo Says These 2 Beaten-Down Cybersecurity Stocks Could Rebound to $200
data.description:
Generative and agentic AI tech has proven to be a headwind for software stocks, and cybersecurity names have been affected too. That impact got more pronounced recently.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The negative catalyst here was Anthropic’s release of Claude Code Security, a new tool in the Claude AI model. It allows the AI to scan codebases – and also to tag vulnerabilities and inform the human users of potential patches. Cybersecurity stocks dropped sharply on the new tool’s release, based on the theory of ‘unload stocks first and sort out the disruption later.’

Even though the overall cybersecurity sector took a hit, and is down generally, the stock analysts at Wells Fargo see options in this beaten-down sector as ripe for a rebound. In fact, they’re predicting that two beaten-down cybersec shares will bounce back to $200 in the coming months.

Using the TipRanks platform, we can see that both of these Wells Fargo picks hold Strong Buy consensus ratings from the Street’s analysts. Let’s give them a closer look and find out just what Wells Fargo sees in them.

Zscaler (ZS)

We’ll start with Zscaler, a cloud-focused cybersec company that has been in operation since 2007. Zscaler’s approach to cybersecurity is based on ‘zero trust,’ an approach that treats everything as a potential threat and provides secure protection for both apps and data. The company makes extensive use of AI technology and leverages that to give real-time network security and extends that protection to IoT and OT. Zscaler’s Zero Trust platform is designed for enterprise use; users can set up unified security efforts without having to interlock various disparate security measures.

Better for the user, Zscaler’s platform is fully scalable and relevant to any private organization. The company’s customers can do away with separate network firewalls, using the one Zscaler system as overall protection. The platform allows for management and supervision of secured devices, classification of new devices, control over access and use, and recognition of incoming threats.

Zero Trust provides several advantages, including superior system protection, increased agility and productivity, and reduced complexity and costs. Zscaler boasts that it secures over 500 billion transactions every day and that some 40% of the Global 2000 companies use the platform.

In the company’s fiscal 2Q26 earnings release, Zscaler reported $815.8 million in revenue, a total that was up 26% year-over-year and was $16.8 million better than had been expected. The bottom line was $1.01 per share by non-GAAP calculations; this beat the forecast by 11 cents per share. The company realized free cash flow in the quarter of $169.1 million, up from $143.4 million year-over-year and reaching 21% of total revenue.

The shares still dropped following the readout as investors were expecting more upside and have been concerned about organic growth. Add in the worries about AI’s effect on the broader software segment and the shares are down by 31% year-to-date.

However, Wells Fargo analyst Richard Poland covers this stock, and he sees it as a sound pick at current prices. Poland writes, “We believe ZS has an entrenched position in the large enterprise space, already having captured 45% of the F500, 40% of the G2K and 20% of enterprises >2,000 employees… Ultimately, we believe new logos can continue to contribute $300-400M per year on ~700 new logos and view saturation concerns, even on a logo basis, as overstated… ZS trades at ~6x EV/ S & ~19x EV/FCF on our CY26 estimates, a discount to the peer group. We see a favorable risk/reward, as momentum is building across several growth pillars that we believe can drive upward estimate revisions in the coming years. If ZS can prove sustainable growth >20%, we expect the valuation gap to the peer group to close.”

Along with his Overweight (Buy) rating here, Poland sets a $200 price target that implies a one-year upside potential of 35% for the stock. (To watch Nowinski’s track record, click here.)

The Street’s consensus on ZS is a Strong Buy, based on 34 recent reviews that break down 30 to 4 in favor of Buy over Hold. Zscaler’s $154.67 trading price and $232.55 average price target indicate room for a gain of 50% in the coming 12 months. (See ZS stock forecast.)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: Archer Aviation Shares Crash Post-Earnings – Here’s What Needham Thinks Comes Next
data.description:
Archer Aviation (NYSE:ACHR) shares look set for a beating in Tuesday’s session following the company’s Q4 results. The readout offered a bigger-than-expected EPS loss partly driven by higher personnel and vendor-related costs. The company saw out FY25 with roughly $2.0 billion in liquidity and emphasized that it remains disciplined in managing its spending.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Since the eVTOL maker is still pre-revenue, it’s all about catalysts ahead, and looking forward to the rest of the year, Needham analyst Chris Pierce thinks the “bullish scenarios for ’26 are easily visible.”

The updated Midnight aircraft is progressing toward piloted transition flights, a key milestone in validating this new transportation tech – something ACHR’s Midnight was unable to demonstrate in 2025, thereby “dampening investor enthusiasm.” Succeeding here could pave the way for a shift in sentiment, while flights under the FAA’s eVTOL eIPP (Integration Pilot Program) should further boost investor confidence ahead of the LA Summer Olympics in 2028. There, ACHR will serve as the official air taxi provider, and the event should represent a “full coming out party for the industry, with certification potentially in the rearview.”

Additionally, ACHR’s $2 billion cash balance underscores its position as a long-term industry leader, providing the flexibility to navigate potential delays, whether company-specific or affecting the broader sector. The company’s spending so far highlights how critical funding is to getting an eVTOL player operational. “We see an industry coalescing around a handful of leaders, with cash and cash burn lowering the threat of new entrants,” Pierce went on to say.

Yet the analyst concedes that all of that is unlikely to convince ACHR bears, who will recall having heard all this before. ACHR previously indicated on its 1Q25 earnings call last May that piloted flight would occur “in the coming days,” and while the company eventually delivered, it was a piloted CTOL (conventional takeoff and landing) flight, which fell short of investor expectations for a piloted eVTOL transition flight. Now, according to the language in the Q4 shareholder letter, ACHR is targeting piloted transition flight “in the coming months,” which could serve as a “bullish catalyst.” However, Pierce anticipates “elevated skepticism” from the bears regarding this claim.

Pierce’s updated model pushes back aircraft production amid higher financial losses, which raises the company’s capital needs. He now expects ACHR to raise $500 million across FY26 and FY27, leading to greater dilution than previously anticipated.

Accordingly, the analyst lowered his price target from $10 to $9, suggesting the stock will gain 34% in the months ahead. Pierce’s rating stays a Buy. (To watch Pierce’s track record, click here)

2 other analysts join Pierce in the bull camp, while an additional 2 Holds add up to a Moderate Buy consensus rating. There are ample gains projected here; at $13.33, the average target implies shares will climb by 98% over the one-year timeframe. (See ACHR stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Middle East War Merits a Price Target Hike for Palantir Stock, Says Rosenblatt
data.description:
War, what is it good for? Well, in Palantir’s (NASDAQ:PLTR) case it appears to be good enough for a new price target.

Claim 70% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

At least in the opinion of Rosenblatt analyst John McPeake. “War regrettably underscores the value of Palantir over just another LLM,” said the analyst, raising his price target from $150 to $200, now suggesting the shares will gain 41% over the coming months. Naturally, McPeake’s rating stays a Buy. (To watch McPeake’s track record, click here)

Over the past few days, the situation evolved rapidly, with several developments unfolding in quick succession. On Feb 26, Anthropic stated that its models should not be deployed in fully autonomous weapons systems. A day later, on Feb 27, the Trump administration directed all U.S. government agencies to cease using Anthropic’s AI technologies. Then, at 09:45 Tehran time (01:15 ET) on Feb 28, Operation Epic Fury began.

The U.S. government has mandated a six-month phase-out of Anthropic’s large language models, which McPeake thinks provides “ample time to switch over to one of the many other LLMs that are supported by Palantir.” The analyst believes heightened global instability and the growing demand for “comprehensive wartime solutions” could drive additional agreements similar to the July U.S. Army deal that consolidated 75 separate contracts into a single arrangement with Palantir. McPeake also views data analysis as the primary LLM performance requirement in theater-of-war settings, and in that respect, he thinks there are “adequate alternatives” for Anthropic’s Claude.

No Palantir discussion can be concluded without a look at its famous valuation. McPeake’s initial $150 price target was based on a 0.9x PEG multiple applied to his Street-high 2027 estimates, with the new price target raising the PEG estimate to 1.2x (88x 2027 EPS). Here, McPeake believes a “premium valuation is more than warranted.”

When comparing Palantir’s revenue trajectory with that of the most successful tech companies at a similar stage, McPeake points out that its projected growth stands out. Based on its 2026 guide, Palantir is expected to grow faster in year 8 than any of the major long-term value creators did at a comparable size. The analyst also thinks it is worth noting that some of those companies – such as Facebook and Google at the time – benefited from transformative acquisitions, while Palantir’s expansion to date has been entirely organic.

So, that’s the Rosenblatt view, but what does the rest of the Street make of Palantir’s prospects? With an additional 13 Buys, 4 Holds and 2 Sells, the analyst consensus rates the stock a Moderate Buy. At $191.76, the average target offers 12-month upside of 35%. (See Palantir stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘Sell-Off Is Overdone,’ Says RBC About AeroVironment Stock
data.description:
AeroVironment (NASDAQ:AVAV) was among a group of defense stocks to surge in early Monday’s trading following the military escalation between the US/Israel and Iran but those gains quickly evaporated with the stock seeing out the session 17% into the red.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

The reversal came after a Space News report indicated that the DoW plans to open the SCAR (Satellite Communications Augmentation Resource) program to new bids, aiming to diversify suppliers and potentially reduce costs. As a result, the drone maker could potentially lose its roughly $1.4 billion contract with the U.S. Space Force. That contract was originally awarded to BlueHalo, an AeroVironment subsidiary acquired last year.

RBC’s Kenneth Herbert, an analyst ranked among the top 1% on Wall Street, concedes that the SCAR program has “hurt credibility,” but he thinks the reaction was excessive.

Nevertheless, Herbert admits the “outlook for the SCAR program is much more in doubt.” That said, the company still anticipates successfully renegotiating at least part of the program by around May, which he thinks would be “incrementally positive” for the stock. Herbert currently reckons that the fiscal year 2026 revenue at risk due to the stop work order is roughly $100 million, based on a full-year run rate of about $140 million, since the program was weighted toward the second half of the year. The analyst expects the company to revise its FY26 full-year guidance to account for the absence of second-half SCAR program revenues. Additionally, a substantial portion of the approximately $1.4 billion in unfunded backlog tied to the program is likely to be removed. However, Herbert stays onside here. “We remain bullish on AVAV considering the unmanned opportunity, and believe the core of the business remains sound,” the 5-star analyst went on to add.

Still, Herbert thinks management should take a more proactive approach in shaping the narrative around the SCAR program. The ambiguity surrounding “backlog definition” in the fiscal second-quarter 2026 results contributed to “investor uncertainty,” and recent developments in the program have reinforced the perception that management may not have full visibility into the business. Herbert thinks the stock would benefit from a pre-announcement ahead of the fiscal third-quarter 2026 results next week (March 10), giving AVAV the opportunity to clarify the SCAR program’s impact on backlog, the balance sheet, and 2H26 financial results. “We believe this would provide the framework for a more constructive discussion on the outlook and potential sources of upside,” Herbert summed up.

To this end, Herbert maintained an Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares although his price target goes from $375 to $325. Nevertheless, there’s still upside of 56% from current levels. (To watch Herbert’s track record, click here)

Most analysts agree with that take; the stock claims a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on a mix of 14 Buys and 1 Hold and Sell, each. At $369.50, the average target implies shares will climb by 77% in the months ahead. (See AVAV stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Buy AMD Shares Ahead of 2H26 Ramp, Says UBS
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) has already improved its AI chip positioning considerably after nabbing deals with some of the leading AI companies, but the semi giant looks poised to add another string to its bow.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

That is the conclusion reached by UBS’s Timothy Arcuri, an analyst ranked in 5th spot among the thousands of Street experts, following a semiconductor bus tour – i.e., a meeting with company representatives. “We emerged more confident in revenue upside for C2027 as the company is now suggesting there could be a third GW-scale customer beyond the deals it has struck with OpenAI and META. While we can’t be sure who this is, MSFT seems to us the most likely,” said the 5-star analyst.

The conversation also touched on investor concerns that the warrant structure and 10% equity stakes granted to Meta and OpenAI could become standard requirements for gigawatt-scale partnerships, with AMD firmly rejecting that notion. The company characterized those arrangements as “unique deals” designed specifically for large language model developers, given the degree of control they exert over the broader ecosystem, and emphasized that it intends to be highly selective and does not plan to extend similar structures to all customers. AMD further argued that the structure should be accretive over the long term and sees it as a mechanism to significantly speed up its market share expansion across the wider accelerator market.

The company also believes the long-term operating margin of its GPU business is closer to 35%, rather than 25%, and sees the first two big deals as scale-building initiatives that enhance long-term economic value. The Meta warrants, similar to the OpenAI warrants, also include additional technical provisions that require the customer to ready its software stack, rather than simply accept product shipments.

Elsewhere, AMD was “very bullish” on the CPU business, noting that Server CPU market growth has exceeded expectations. The November Analyst Day projections were worked on in summer 2025, and since then demand indicators have “continued to strengthen.” As a result, AMD now sees potential upside to its previously outlined $60 billion 2030 market opportunity and 18% five-year CAGR, with growth supported by both higher unit volumes and stronger ASPs.

The company expects CPU demand to benefit from AI-related workloads as well as traditional enterprise applications, alongside an improving pricing backdrop. Historically, AMD assumed that rising core counts would be offset by declining ASP per core, but it now anticipates ASP per core to remain broadly flat rather than trending downward.

While Arcuri came away confident in AMD’s prospects, he thinks it is “more of a 2H:26 story,” as MI450 shipments tied to the OpenAI deal begin, with Meta-related shipments expected to follow shortly thereafter.

Bottom line, Arcuri maintained a Buy rating on the shares, backed by a $310 price target that points toward 12-month returns of 56%. (To watch Arcuri’s track record, click here)

22 other analysts are also AMD bulls, while 8 Holds add up to a Moderate Buy consensus rating. The forecast calls for one-year returns of 44%, considering the average target clocks in at $286.04. (See AMD stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel
